figures.caption: Fig. 2 Visualization of global expert assignments across datasets in latent space. Each dot represents the assignment of an image to a global expert. The high-dimensional assignment vectors are projected into 2D using MDS for visualization. Left: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) shows noisy expert usage across domains. Right: HMR-Net produces structured clusters, where global experts consistently specialize based on dataset characteristics.

Fig. 10 Evaluation of router network representation collapse. In the resulting distributions, lighter regions indicate higher feature attention. The heatmaps having uniform intensity suggest improved balance in expert selection and reduced likelihood of representational collapse.

Fig. 12 The distribution of expert utilization across different layers (layers 1, 3). The visualisation of the distribution of expert utilisation for MoE variants. Our HMR-Net shows more intense specialisation. (even in the early layer): each dataset is consistently routed to its assigned experts, leading to interpretable and stable specialisation without auxiliary balancing losses. In MoE, the routing distributes samples uniformly across experts, resulting in flat usage patterns with no clear dataset-expert alignment across layers.

Fig. 11 ROC curves on the DIOR and DOTA. The results illustrating the impact of the CEKD, Complexity Estimation (CE) and global distillation (GD) modules. The full model outperforms all ablated variants, confirming the complementary benefits of all modules.
Table 7 Cross-dataset generalization on the xView.
Table 6 Cross-dataset generalization on the DOTA.

Fig. 1 Comparison between standard Mixture of Experts (MOE) and our Hierarchical Modular Routing. Unlike conventional models with global-only expert assignment, our method introduces dataset-specific global routing and region-level local specialization.
Table 8 Cross-dataset generalization on NWPU VHR-10.
Fig. 7 Qualitative results for unseen objects. Our method detects novel categories with improved precision and semantic alignment, compared to CastDet.
Table 2 Per-dataset comparison on the DOTA.
Table 9 Comparison with state-of-the-art methods under ZSD setting on the DIOR and DOTA. Performance is reported in terms of RE@100 at multiple IoU thresholds and mAP.

Fig. 8 Visualization results for Zero-shot inference. In both examples, ViLD fails to distinguish the referent structure, while CastDet and our model with bipartite localize partial entities. Our model with Hungarian algorithm (HA) shows finegrained attention and accurately interprets the entire prompt. Objects like “solar panel” or “playground” are not labeled in training, but are localized through our textual alignment process.

Fig. 14 Performance vs computation for varying number of alignments. The blue line indicates mAP and the red line shows relative inference time range. The shaded band represents min–max overhead across multiple runs. By setting the number of alignments to 16 per image, accuracy is improved significantly with minor latency increase.

Fig. 3 Overall architecture of the proposed framework. Each image is processed by a single global expert, while all global experts also contribute to a teacher ensemble that supervises the selected expert via cross-expert knowledge distillation. The routed global output is then parsed into spatial regions by a complexity-aware local routing module, where region-toexpert assignments enable fine-grained specialization. The outputs are aggregated and passed to detection heads, with an optional Expert Module supporting open-category detection.
Table 4 Per-dataset evaluation on NWPU VHR-10.

Fig. 4 Conditional Expert Module: RU-level visual embeddings are aligned to textual prompts via bipartite matching, enabling prompt-driven open-category detection.

Fig. 13 Impact of the number of global experts EG when training on the 4-dataset mixture, reported on DOTA. (a) mAP as the number of global experts increases. (b) Expert utilization across different expert configurations. The routing distribution becomes more balanced as the number of experts increases, with all experts actively contributing in the six-expert setting.
Fig. 9 Comparison of feature maps generated by global routing, local routing, and their combined approach.
